content.assessment: Finland's experience directly validates the value of decomposition analysis: post-2010 data show identification growth concentrated in the newly created 'intensified support' tier while the top tier remained flat, a pattern only visible because of consistent longitudinal categorization. This suggests reclassification effects, not just true prevalence change, can masquerade as headline growth — precisely the ambiguity TP-live-t2 aims to resolve for Hungary's divergent SNI/BTMN series. Finland's regional variation data (4%-13%) further show that national aggregates conceal locally driven administrative differences, reinforcing the case for granular, reconciled data architecture.
opportunities: Hungary could adopt Finland's before/after tier-level decomposition approach around its own 2018/2020 rule changes to identify reclassification versus true prevalence shifts
strengths: Finland's tier-specific breakdown (flat special-support tier, rising intensified-support tier) is a proof-of-concept that reconciled, disaggregated data can distinguish reclassification from genuine prevalence change
Documented persistent regional variation in Finland after reform validates the need for sub-national breakdowns within any reconciled monitoring architecture
threats: As in Finland, reconciled data may reveal that municipal/county-level variation is driven by administrative and fiscal incentives rather than need, which is politically sensitive to publish
weaknesses: Finland's own case shows that even with harmonized categories, regional/municipal interpretation differences (4%-13%) persist and are not resolved by better measurement alone, so reconciliation may clarify but not eliminate definitional ambiguity
